question_text: Let n be a fixed positive integer and R be the relation in Z defined as a R b if and only if a – b is divisible by n, {tex}\forall a, b \in {Z}{/tex} . Show that R is an equivalence relation.
question_html: Let n be a fixed positive integer and R be the relation in Z defined as a R b if and only if a – b is divisible by n, {tex}\forall a, b \in {Z}{/tex}. Show that R is an equivalence relation.
solution_text: Since {tex}a \mathrm{R} a, \forall a \in \mathrm{Z}, \mathrm{a}{/tex} , and because 0 is divisible by n, therefore R is reflexive. a R b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b is divisible by n, then b - a, is divisible by n, so b R a. Hence R is symmetric. Let a R b and b R c, for a,b,c, {tex}\in{/tex} Z. Then a - b = np, b - c = nq, for some p, q {tex}\in{/tex} Z. Therefore, a - c = n (p + q) and so a R c. Hence R is reflexive and so equivalence relation.
solution_html:
1. Since {tex}a \mathrm{R} a, \forall a \in \mathrm{Z}, \mathrm{a}{/tex}, and because 0 is divisible by n, therefore R is reflexive.
  2. a R b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b is divisible by n, then b - a, is divisible by n, so b R a.
    Hence R is symmetric.
  3. Let a R b and b R c, for a,b,c, {tex}\in{/tex} Z. Then a - b = np, b - c = nq, for some p, q {tex}\in{/tex} Z.
    Therefore, a - c = n (p + q) and so a R c.
    Hence R is reflexive and so equivalence relation.

question_text: Solve for x, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ x+6 & x-1 & x+2 \\ x-1 & x+2 & x+6 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
question_html: Solve for x, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ x+6 & x-1 & x+2 \\ x-1 & x+2 & x+6 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
solution_text: Given {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ x+6 & x-1 & x+2 \\ x-1 & x+2 & x+6 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} Using {tex}\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\\ &\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{1} \end{aligned}{/tex} , we get {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ 4 & -7 & 3 \\ -3 & -4 & 7 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} Using {tex}\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{C}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{1}\\ &\mathrm{C}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{3}-\mathrm{C}_{1} \end{aligned},{/tex} we get {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+2 & 4 & -3 \\ 4 & -11 & -1 \\ -3 & -1 & 10 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} Therefore, (x + 2) (-111) -4(37) -3(-37) = 0 which on solving gives {tex}x=-\frac{7}{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ x+6 & x-1 & x+2 \\ x-1 & x+2 & x+6 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
Using {tex}\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\\ &\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{1} \end{aligned}{/tex}, we get {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+2 & x+6 & x-1 \\ 4 & -7 & 3 \\ -3 & -4 & 7 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
Using {tex}\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{C}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{1}\\ &\mathrm{C}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{3}-\mathrm{C}_{1} \end{aligned},{/tex} we get {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+2 & 4 & -3 \\ 4 & -11 & -1 \\ -3 & -1 & 10 \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}
Therefore, (x + 2) (-111) -4(37) -3(-37) = 0
which on solving gives {tex}x=-\frac{7}{3}{/tex}.

question_text: If {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right){/tex} and {tex}B=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right){/tex} , verify that (AB)' = B'A'.
question_html: If {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right){/tex} and {tex}B=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right){/tex}, verify that (AB)' = B'A'.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{AB}=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 3 & -4 \\ 15 & 5 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{LHS}=(\mathrm{AB})^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 7 & 15 \\ 3 & 5 \\ -4 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex} RHS {tex}=\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 3 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 2 & -3 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lc} 7 & 15 \\ 3 & 5 \\ -4 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex} Hence LHS = RHS
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{AB}=\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 3 & -4 \\ 15 & 5 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\mathrm{LHS}=(\mathrm{AB})^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 7 & 15 \\ 3 & 5 \\ -4 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex}
RHS {tex}=\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 3 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 2 & -3 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lc} 7 & 15 \\ 3 & 5 \\ -4 & -6 \end{array}\right){/tex} 
Hence LHS = RHS

question_text: Determine the value of k so that the function: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{k . \text { cos } 2 x}{\pi-4 x}, & \text { if } x \neq \frac{\pi}{4} \\ 5, & \text { if } x=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
question_html:
Determine the value of k so that the function:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{k . \text { cos } 2 x}{\pi-4 x}, & \text { if } x \neq \frac{\pi}{4} \\ 5, & \text { if } x=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_text: Since f is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , we have {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} f(x)=5{/tex} Now, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{k \cdot \cos 2 x}{\pi-4 x}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{k \cos 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-y\right)}{\pi-4\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-y\right)}{/tex} , where {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-x=y,{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{k \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 y\right)}{\pi-\pi+4 y}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{(k \sin 2 y)}{2 \cdot 2 y}=\frac{k}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{k}{2}=5 \Rightarrow k=10{/tex}
solution_html:
Since f is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, we have {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} f(x)=5{/tex}
Now, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{k \cdot \cos 2 x}{\pi-4 x}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{k \cos 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-y\right)}{\pi-4\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-y\right)}{/tex}, where {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-x=y,{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{k \cdot \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 y\right)}{\pi-\pi+4 y}=\lim \limits_{y \rightarrow 0} \frac{(k \sin 2 y)}{2 \cdot 2 y}=\frac{k}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{k}{2}=5 \Rightarrow k=10{/tex}

question_text: If y = {tex}e^{a \cos ^{-1} x}{/tex} , show that {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d^{2} x}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex} .
question_html: If y = {tex}e^{a \cos ^{-1} x}{/tex}, show that {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d^{2} x}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}y=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \frac{(-a)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-a y{/tex} ... (i) Differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=-a \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = – a (-ay) [from i] Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \frac{(-a)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-a y{/tex} ... (i)
Differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=-a \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
= – a (-ay) [from i]
Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve x = sin3t, y = cos 2t at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve x = sin3t, y = cos 2t at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=+3 \cos 3 t, \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 \sin 2 t{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 \sin 2 t}{3 \cos 3 t}, {/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{3 \cos 3 \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{-2}{3 \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex} Also x = sin 3t {tex}=\sin 3 \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and y = cos2t {tex}=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0{/tex} Therefore, Point is {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0\right){/tex} Hence, equation of tangent is y - 0 {tex}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\left(x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}2 \sqrt{2} x-3 y-2=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=+3 \cos 3 t, \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 \sin 2 t{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{2 \sin 2 t}{3 \cos 3 t}, {/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}}{3 \cos 3 \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{-2}{3 \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}
Also x = sin 3t {tex}=\sin 3 \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and y = cos2t {tex}=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0{/tex}
Therefore, Point is {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0\right){/tex}
Hence, equation of tangent is y - 0 {tex}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}\left(x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}2 \sqrt{2} x-3 y-2=0{/tex}

question_text: Find the intervals in which the function f (x) = sin 4 x + cos 4 x, 0 < x < {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
question_html: Find the intervals in which the function f (x) = sin4 x + cos4 x, 0 < x < {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.
solution_text: f'(x) = 4sin 3 x cosx - 4cos 3 x sin = -4 sinx cosx (cos 2 x - sin 2 x) = -sin 4x. Therefore, f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x=n \pi \Rightarrow x=n \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} Now, for 0 < x < {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} f'(x) < 0 Therefore, f is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} Similarly, we can show that f is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
f'(x) = 4sin3x cosx - 4cos3x sin
= -4 sinx cosx (cos2x - sin2x)
= -sin 4x. Therefore,
f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 4 x=n \pi \Rightarrow x=n \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
Now, for 0 < x < {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
f'(x) < 0
Therefore, f is strictly decreasing in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
Similarly, we can show that f is strictly increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x \cos ^{3} x d x{/tex}
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x \cos ^{3} x d x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x \cos ^{3} x d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right) \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}} t^{4}\left(1-t^{2}\right) d t{/tex} where sin x = t {tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(t^{4}-t^{6}\right) d t=\left[\frac{t^{5}}{5}-\frac{t^{7}}{7}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5}-\frac{1}{7}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{7}=\frac{1}{32}\left(\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{28}\right)=\frac{23}{4480}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x \cos ^{3} x d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sin ^{4} x\left(1-\sin ^{2} x\right) \cos x d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}} t^{4}\left(1-t^{2}\right) d t{/tex} where sin x = t
{tex}=\int \limits_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(t^{4}-t^{6}\right) d t=\left[\frac{t^{5}}{5}-\frac{t^{7}}{7}\right]_{0}^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5}-\frac{1}{7}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{7}=\frac{1}{32}\left(\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{28}\right)=\frac{23}{4480}{/tex}

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac{3 x+1}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x{/tex}
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int \frac{3 x+1}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+1}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x=\int \frac{\frac{3}{4}(4 x-2)+\frac{5}{2}}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{4 x-2}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x+\frac{5}{4} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}-x+\frac{3}{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{5}{4} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{5}{4} \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{5}}+c{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{5}}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int \frac{3 x+1}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x=\int \frac{\frac{3}{4}(4 x-2)+\frac{5}{2}}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{4 x-2}{2 x^{2}-2 x+3} d x+\frac{5}{4} \int \frac{1}{x^{2}-x+\frac{3}{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{5}{4} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{5}{4} \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{5}}+c{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}-2 x+3\right|+\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{5}}+c{/tex}

question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int x \cdot(\log x)^{2} d x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}I=\int x(\log x)^{2} \cdot d x=\int(\log x)^{2} x d x{/tex} {tex}=(\log x)^{2} \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int 2 \log x \frac{1}{x} \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}(\log x)^{2}-\int \log x \cdot x d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}(\log x)^{2}-\left[\log x \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\int \frac{1}{x} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{2} d x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x^{2}}{2}(\log x)^{2}-\frac{x^{2}}{2} \log x+\frac{x^{2}}{4}+c{/tex}

question_text: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x+\left(y-2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d y=0{/tex} , given that x = 0 when y = 1.
question_html: Find a particular solution of the differential equation {tex}2 y e^{\frac{x}{y}} d x+\left(y-2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}\right) d y=0{/tex}, given that x = 0 when y = 1.
solution_text: Given differential equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y \cdot e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} Putting {tex}\frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow x=v y \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v y e^{v}-y}{2 y e^{v}}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex} {tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex} Hence {tex}2 e^{v} d v=-\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 e^{v}=-\log |y|+c{/tex} or {tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}=-\log |y|+c{/tex} when x = 0, y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 2 Therefore, the particular solution is {tex}2{e^{\frac{x}{y}}} = - \log \left| y \right| + 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x e^{\frac{x}{y}}-y}{2 y \cdot e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\frac{x}{y}=v \Rightarrow x=v y \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=v+y \frac{d v}{d y}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}v+y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v y e^{v}-y}{2 y e^{v}}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}{/tex}
{tex}y \frac{d v}{d y}=\frac{2 v e^{v}-1}{2 e^{v}}-v{/tex}
Hence {tex}2 e^{v} d v=-\frac{d y}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 e^{v}=-\log |y|+c{/tex}
or {tex}2 e^{\frac{x}{y}}=-\log |y|+c{/tex}
when x = 0, y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C =  2
Therefore, the particular solution is {tex}2{e^{\frac{x}{y}}} = - \log \left| y \right| + 2{/tex}

question_html: Determine the vector equation of a line passing through (1, 2, –4) and perpendicular to the two lines {tex}\vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: There are three coins. One is a biased coin that comes up with tail 60% of the times, the second is also a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the times and the third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it showed heads. What is the probability that it was the unbiased coin?
question_html: There are three coins. One is a biased coin that comes up with tail 60% of the times, the second is also a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the times and the third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it showed heads. What is the probability that it was the unbiased coin?
solution_text: Let E 1 : selection of first (biased) coin E 2 : selection of second (biased) coin E 3 : selection of third (unbiased) coin {tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=P\left(E_{2}\right)=P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let A denote the event of getting a head Therefore, {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{E_{1}}\right)=\frac{40}{100}, \quad P\left(\frac{A}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{75}{100}, P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}P\left(\frac{E_{3}}{A}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{1}}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{2}}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{40}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{75}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}=\frac{10}{33}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1: selection of first (biased) coin
E2: selection of second (biased) coin
E3 : selection of third (unbiased) coin
{tex}P\left(E_{1}\right)=P\left(E_{2}\right)=P\left(E_{3}\right)=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Let A denote the event of getting a head
Therefore, {tex}P\left(\frac{A}{E_{1}}\right)=\frac{40}{100}, \quad P\left(\frac{A}{E_{2}}\right)=\frac{75}{100}, P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(\frac{E_{3}}{A}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{1}}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{2}}\right)+P\left(E_{3}\right) P\left(\frac{A}{E_{3}}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{40}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{75}{100}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}=\frac{10}{33}{/tex}

question_text: Find A -1 , where {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 4 & 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right){/tex} . Hence solve the following system of equations 4x + 2y + 3z = 2, x + y + z = 1, 3x + y - 2z = 5.
question_html: Find A-1, where {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 4 & 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right){/tex}. Hence solve the following system of equations 4x + 2y + 3z = 2, x + y + z = 1, 3x + y - 2z = 5.
solution_text: {tex}|A|={/tex} 4(-3) -1(-7) + 3(-1) = -12 + 7 - 3 = -8 A 11 = -3, A 12 = 7, A 13 = -1 A 21 = 5, A 22 = -17, A 23 = -1 Therefore, {tex}A^{-1}=-\frac{1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{rrr} -3 & 5 & -2 \\ 7 & -17 & 2 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right){/tex} Given equation can be written as {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime} \cdot X=B \Rightarrow X=\left(A^{\prime}\right) B{/tex} = (A -1 )'B {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right)=\frac{-1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 7 & -1 \\ 5 & -17 & -1 \\ -2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & +7 & -5= & -4 \\ 10 & -17 & -5= & -12 \\ -4 & +2 & +10 & = & 8 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{c} \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{3}{2} \\ -1 \end{array}\right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}, y=\frac{3}{2}, \quad z=-1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}|A|={/tex} 4(-3) -1(-7) + 3(-1) = -12 + 7 - 3 = -8
A11 = -3, A12 = 7, A13 = -1
A21 = 5, A22 = -17, A23 = -1
Therefore, {tex}A^{-1}=-\frac{1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{rrr} -3 & 5 & -2 \\ 7 & -17 & 2 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right){/tex}
Given equation can be written as
{tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime} \cdot X=B \Rightarrow X=\left(A^{\prime}\right) B{/tex}
= (A-1)'B
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right)=\frac{-1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} -3 & 7 & -1 \\ 5 & -17 & -1 \\ -2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \\ 5 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{8}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & +7 & -5= & -4 \\ 10 & -17 & -5= & -12 \\ -4 & +2 & +10 & = & 8 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{c} \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{3}{2} \\ -1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
Therefore, {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}, y=\frac{3}{2}, \quad z=-1{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Using elementary transformations, find A -1 , where {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
question_html:
Using elementary transformations, find A-1, where
{tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
solution_text: {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right) A{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+\mathrm{R}_{1} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 5 & -2 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+2 \mathrm{R}_{3} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right) \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}+2 \mathrm{R}_{2} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+2 \mathrm{R}_{3} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 4 & 10 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right) \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ -1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right) A{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+\mathrm{R}_{1} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 5 & -2 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex}{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+2 \mathrm{R}_{3} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right){/tex}{tex}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right) \mathrm{A}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}+2 \mathrm{R}_{2} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+2 \mathrm{R}_{3} \Rightarrow\left(\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 4 & 10 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right) \mathrm{A}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\left(\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 5 \end{array}\right){/tex}

question_text: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of maximum volume and of given slant height is {tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2}{/tex} .
question_html: Show that the semi-vertical angle of the cone of maximum volume and of given slant height is {tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2}{/tex}.
solution_text: Volume {tex}v=v=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex} l 2 = h 2 + r 2 {tex}v=\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(R-h^{2}\right) h=\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(P h-h^{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d h}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(l^{2}-3 h^{2}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}l=\sqrt{3} h, \quad r=\sqrt{2} h{/tex} {tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{r}{h}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} v}{d h^{2}}=-2 \pi h<0{/tex} Therefore, v is maximum.
solution_html:
Volume {tex}v=v=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h{/tex}

l2 = h2 + r2
{tex}v=\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(R-h^{2}\right) h=\frac{1}{3} \pi\left(P h-h^{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d v}{d h}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left(l^{2}-3 h^{2}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}l=\sqrt{3} h, \quad r=\sqrt{2} h{/tex}
{tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{r}{h}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} v}{d h^{2}}=-2 \pi h<0{/tex}
Therefore, v is maximum.

question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int_{1}^{3}\left(3 x^{2}+2 x+5\right) d x{/tex} by the method of limit of sum.
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int_{1}^{3}\left(3 x^{2}+2 x+5\right) d x{/tex} by the method of limit of sum.
solution_text: {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int_{1}^{3}\left(3 x^{2}+2 x+5\right) d x=\int_{1}^{3} f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow o} h[f(1)+f(1+h)+f(1+2 h)+{/tex} .... +f(1 + (n - 1)h] .... (i) where {tex}h=\frac{3-1}{n}=\frac{2}{n}{/tex} Now f(1) 3 + 2 + 5 = 10 f(1 + h) = 3 + 3h 2 + 6h + 2 + 2h + 5 = 10 + 8h + 3h 2 f(1 + 2h) = 3 + 12h 2 + 12h + 2 + 4h + 5 = 10 + 8.2h + 3.2 2 .h 2 f(1 + (n - 1)h = 10 + 8(n - 1)h + 3(n - 1) 2 .h 2 {tex}I=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} h\left[10 n+8 h \frac{n(n-1)}{2}+3 h^{2} \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{n}\left[10 n+\frac{16}{n} \frac{n(n-1)}{2}+\frac{12}{n^{2}} \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{n}\left[10 n+8(n-1) \frac{2}{n}(n-1)(2 n-1)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} 2\left[10+8\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right)+2\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right)\left(2-\frac{1}{n}\right)\right]{/tex} = 2[10 + 8 + 4] = 44
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int_{1}^{3}\left(3 x^{2}+2 x+5\right) d x=\int_{1}^{3} f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow o} h[f(1)+f(1+h)+f(1+2 h)+{/tex} .... +f(1 + (n - 1)h] .... (i)
where {tex}h=\frac{3-1}{n}=\frac{2}{n}{/tex}
Now
f(1) 3 + 2 + 5 = 10
f(1 + h) = 3 + 3h2 + 6h + 2 + 2h + 5 = 10 + 8h + 3h2
f(1 + 2h) = 3 + 12h2 + 12h + 2 + 4h + 5 = 10 + 8.2h + 3.22.h2
f(1 + (n - 1)h = 10 + 8(n - 1)h + 3(n - 1)2.h2
{tex}I=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} h\left[10 n+8 h \frac{n(n-1)}{2}+3 h^{2} \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{n}\left[10 n+\frac{16}{n} \frac{n(n-1)}{2}+\frac{12}{n^{2}} \frac{n(n-1)(2 n-1)}{6}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{n}\left[10 n+8(n-1) \frac{2}{n}(n-1)(2 n-1)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow \infty} 2\left[10+8\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right)+2\left(1-\frac{1}{n}\right)\left(2-\frac{1}{n}\right)\right]{/tex}
= 2[10 + 8 + 4] = 44

question_text: Find the area of the triangle formed by positive x-axis, and the normal and tangent to the circle x 2 + y 2 = 4 at {tex}(1, \sqrt{3}){/tex} , using integration.
question_html: Find the area of the triangle formed by positive x-axis, and the normal and tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = 4 at {tex}(1, \sqrt{3}){/tex}, using integration.
solution_text: Equation of tangent to x 2 + y 2 = 4 at {tex}(1, \sqrt{3}){/tex} is {tex}x+\sqrt{3} y=4{/tex} . Therefore, {tex}y=\frac{4-x}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} Equation of normal {tex}y=\sqrt{3} x{/tex} Therefore, required area {tex}=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{3} x d x+\int_{1}^{4} \frac{4-x}{\sqrt{3}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left(\sqrt{3} \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{1}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left(4 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)^{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[8-\frac{7}{2}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{sq.units}{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of tangent to x2 + y2 = 4 at {tex}(1, \sqrt{3}){/tex} is
{tex}x+\sqrt{3} y=4{/tex}. Therefore, {tex}y=\frac{4-x}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Equation of normal {tex}y=\sqrt{3} x{/tex}
Therefore, required area {tex}=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{3} x d x+\int_{1}^{4} \frac{4-x}{\sqrt{3}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\sqrt{3} \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)_{0}^{1}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left(4 x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right)^{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[8-\frac{7}{2}\right]=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}=2 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{sq.units}{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the plane through the intersection of the planes x + 3 y + 6 = 0 and 3x - y - 4z = 0 and whose perpendicular distance from the origin is unity.

solution_text: Equation of required plane is (x + 3y + 6) + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (3x - y - 4z) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad(1+3 \lambda) x+(3-\lambda) y-4 \lambda z+6=0{/tex} Perpendicular distance to the plane from origin is Therefore, {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{(1+3 \lambda)^{2}+(3-\lambda)^{2}+(-4 \lambda)^{2}}}=1{/tex} or {tex}36=1+9 \lambda^{2}+6 \lambda+9+\lambda^{2}-6 \lambda+16 \lambda^{2}{/tex} or {tex}26 \lambda^{2}=26 \Rightarrow \lambda=\pm 1{/tex} Equations of required planes are 4x + 2y - 4z + 6 = 0 and -2x + 4y + 4z + 6 = 0 or 2x + y - 2z + 3 = 0 and x - 2y - 2z - 3 = 0

question_text: Find the distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from the plane x + y + z = 2 measured parallel to the line 2 x = y = z.
question_html: Find the distance of the point (3, 4, 5) from the plane x + y + z = 2 measured parallel to the line 2 x = y = z.
solution_text: Equation of line is 2x = y = z i.e. {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{1}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{2}{/tex} Equation of line PQ is {tex}\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{z-5}{2}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow Q(\lambda+3,2 \lambda+4,2 \lambda+5){/tex} lies on plane. Therefore, {tex}\lambda+3+2 \lambda+4+2 \lambda+5-2=0{/tex} or {tex}5 \lambda=-10{/tex} gives {tex}\lambda=-2{/tex} which gives the coordinates of Q(1, 0, 1) Therefore, {tex}P Q=\sqrt{4+16+16}{/tex} = 6 units
solution_html:
Equation of line is 2x = y = z i.e. {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{1}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{2}{/tex}

Equation of line PQ is
{tex}\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{z-5}{2}=\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Q(\lambda+3,2 \lambda+4,2 \lambda+5){/tex} lies on plane. Therefore,
{tex}\lambda+3+2 \lambda+4+2 \lambda+5-2=0{/tex}
or {tex}5 \lambda=-10{/tex} gives {tex}\lambda=-2{/tex} which gives the coordinates of Q(1, 0, 1)
Therefore, {tex}P Q=\sqrt{4+16+16}{/tex} = 6 units

question_text: A furniture firm manufactures chairs and tables, each requiring the use of three machines A, B and C. Production of one chair requires 2 hours on machine A, 1 hour on machine B and 1 hour on machine C. Each table requires 1 hour each on machine A and B and 3 hours on machine C. The profit obtained by selling one chair is ₹30 while by selling one table the profit is ₹60. The total time available per week on machine A is 70 hours, on machine B is 40 hours and on machine C is 90 hours. How many chairs and tables should be made per week so as to maximise profit? Formulate the problems as a L.P.P. and solve it graphically.
question_html: A furniture firm manufactures chairs and tables, each requiring the use of three machines A, B and C. Production of one chair requires 2 hours on machine A, 1 hour on machine B and 1 hour on machine C. Each table requires 1 hour each on machine A and B and 3 hours on machine C. The profit obtained by selling one chair is ₹30 while by selling one table the profit is ₹60. The total time available per week on machine A is 70 hours, on machine B is 40 hours and on machine C is 90 hours. How many chairs and tables should be made per week so as to maximise profit? Formulate the problems as a L.P.P. and solve it graphically.
solution_text: Let number of chairs to be made per week be x and tables be y. Thus we have to maximise P = 30 x + 60 y Subject to {tex}x+y \leq 70{/tex} {tex}x+y \leq 40{/tex} {tex}x+3 y \leq 90{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0 y \geq 0{/tex} Vertices of feasible region are A (0,30), B (15, 25), C (30,10), D (35, 0) 12 P (at A) = 30 (60) = 1800 P (at B) = 30 (15 + 50) = 1950
solution_html:
Let number of chairs to be made per week be x and tables be y.
Thus we have to maximise P = 30 x + 60 y
Subject to

{tex}x+y \leq 40{/tex}
{tex}x+3 y \leq 90{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0 y \geq 0{/tex}
Vertices of feasible region are
A (0,30), B (15, 25), C (30,10), D (35, 0) 12
P (at A) = 30 (60) = 1800
P (at B) = 30 (15 + 50) = 1950
